papers.title: DARTCONTROL: A DIFFUSION-BASED AUTOREGRES-SIVE MOTION MODEL FOR REAL-TIME TEXT-DRIVEN MOTION CONTROL

strengths: The paper proposes an autoregressive latent motion primitive representation that strikes a practical balance between temporal coherence and controllability, distinct from prior sequence-level diffusion or frame-level AR models.

Integrates two complementary control mechanisms over the same latent space: gradient-based latent noise optimization and an RL policy that outputs latent diffusion noises as actions, enabling diverse downstream tasks without paired control signals.
Uses a VAE on motion primitives to denoise dataset artifacts and simplify the diffusion target distribution; the observation that this reduces jitter is compelling and well-motivated.

Comprehensive evaluation on text-conditioned temporal composition against strong baselines (FlowMDM, TEACH, DoubleTake, T2M-GPT*), with dataset-level metrics, transition smoothness (jerk), and human preference studies.
Demonstrates strong performance on text-conditioned in-betweening with quantitative comparisons to DNO and OmniControl, including goal error and skating.
RL control is validated against GAMMA with multiple locomotion styles, reporting success rate, skating, and floor distance at >200 FPS.
Clear problem formulation, model diagrams, and concise autoregressive rollout algorithm; the separation of VAE (frozen) and denoiser training is easy to follow.
Method details like scheduled sampling, classifier-free guidance, and canonicalization are explained with sufficient intuition.
Addresses a critical gap: online, real-time, text-driven motion with precise spatial control, where most diffusion approaches are offline and slow.
The latent control view (no paired control data needed) is versatile and can simplify building task-specific controllers in animation/VR/robotics pipelines.
weaknesses: Dependence on frame-aligned action annotations (BABEL) is strong; performance degrades with coarse sentence-level labels (HML3D), limiting broader applicability.
Over-parameterized frame representation mixing rotations and joint positions may risk internal inconsistency; the paper relies on VAE regularization but lacks explicit analysis of consistency errors.
Long-horizon stability and drift are not systematically quantified beyond transition jerk; error accumulation across very long sequences is a practical concern for AR models.
Human-scene interaction results are qualitative; no quantitative collision/contact metrics are reported for these experiments despite available SDF machinery.

RL comparisons are limited (GAMMA only, walking); no head-to-head with recent diffusion-policy character controls or ASE-style skills; generalization to dynamic obstacles or OOD goals is not evaluated.
Latent noise optimization cost is not profiled; scalability to complex scenes/long horizons could be computationally heavy due to backpropagation through diffusion and the decoder.
Some crucial training details (e.g., exact loss weights, history/primitive ablations) are deferred to appendices; the main paper would benefit from summarizing key ablation takeaways.
The canonicalization steps during rollout and their effect on global trajectory drift could be spelled out more explicitly.

While related prior art is broadly covered, empirical comparisons to other real-time/AR diffusion character control methods (e.g., Shi et al. 2024, Chen et al. 2024) are absent; even if text is unsupported there, speed/quality comparisons could be informative.
Quantitative evaluation on additional temporal composition baselines that are online would strengthen claims about real-time superiority and semantic alignment.

papers.title: TRANSFORMERS PROVABLY SOLVE PARITY EFFICIENTLY WITH CHAIN OF THOUGHT

strengths: Extends known impossibility results for gradient-based learning of parity to a finite-sample setting with approximate gradients, bridging a gap between population-level theory and realistic training signals.

Provides the first (to my knowledge) optimization analysis showing that training a transformer to generate CoT can provably recover a nontrivial compositional computation (parity) with strong iteration guarantees.

Introduces a constructive end-to-end CoT training analysis without teacher forcing via a consistency-checking mechanism and block autoregressivity that compresses the chain length to logarithmic depth.

The gradient-signal analysis at initialization that isolates “interaction” terms and shows dominance of relevant child nodes is technically insightful and connects well to why intermediate supervision helps.

Synthetic experiments, while modest, are well aligned with the theory and clearly exhibit the predicted qualitative phenomena: direct training stalls, CoT with teacher forcing converges stably, and CoT with self-consistency exhibits phased learning by levels.
The ablation via four training regimes (direct, CoT end-to-end, CoT+teacher forcing, CoT+self-consistency) is helpful and maps to the theorems.
The problem setup, architecture simplifications, and causal masks are presented clearly, with helpful diagrams and proof sketches that make the main ideas accessible.
The relation to prior negative results and to RNN-based positive results (Wies et al., 2023) is well articulated.
Offers a concrete, mechanistic account of how CoT-style intermediate supervision can transform a provably hard optimization problem into a tractable one for transformers.
The results inform current practice around process supervision and self-consistency in LLMs, giving principled support for their benefits in multi-step reasoning.
weaknesses: The architecture is heavily idealized: attention depends only on positional encodings (values carry only x, keys/queries do not depend on content), the link function φ is hand-crafted to map averages to parities with special derivative properties, and Theorem 7 relies on weight quantization and a bespoke self-consistency filter. While reasonable for theory, these assumptions reduce realism and raise questions about robustness to standard transformer components (residuals, MLPs, value-dependent attention).

The finite-sample hardness result assumes an approximate gradient oracle that may adversarially perturb gradients. This is standard in prior work but can be seen as stronger than the stochastic noise faced by SGD/mini-batch training, limiting the scope of the negative result.

The teacher-forcing positive result requires n = Ω(d^{2+ε}) samples and a precise gradient accuracy O(d^{-2−ε/8}) with a step size scaling as d^{2+ε/16}. The sensitivity to these scalings and constants is not explored, and it is unclear how tight or necessary these magnitudes are.

Experiments are limited to small synthetic parity (d = 64) and do not explore robustness to architectural deviations (e.g., content-dependent attention), to different φ realizations, or to varying the amount of process supervision and augmentation.
The self-consistency theorem uses integer quantization, but the experiments omit quantization; an experiment validating its role (or showing it is not crucial) would strengthen the empirical case.

Generalization is largely evaluated via training loss reductions; explicit train/test splits and reporting of test accuracy or robustness to longer chains (out-of-distribution k) would add credibility.
Some statements rely on dense asymptotic notation; a brief discussion of constants or practical numbers (e.g., what d, n, and error tolerances correspond to in the plots) would aid interpretation.

The construction of the intermediate labels assumes access to a fixed tree decomposition over the unknown subset S; a short remark on how the decomposition is chosen and whether different valid trees affect learnability would help.

Recent theoretical analyses of gradient signal attenuation for high-order interactions and why explicit CoT helps (e.g., the Order‑r Interaction framework; see Related Work 2601.21576) appear closely connected in spirit to the interaction-term analysis here and deserve discussion.

Empirical architectural approaches that increase effective depth via recurrence across tokens (TurboConn; 2602.17993) show strong parity performance and provide a complementary perspective to CoT; positioning relative to these would be valuable.
Scaling-law views of when CoT helps (2604.08872) frame parity as a pathological, highly nonlocal task; relating this work to that perspective would contextualize the scope of applicability.

papers.title: Recovering Plasticity of Neural Networks Via Soft Weight Rescaling

strengths: The “proportionality” construction is a clean articulation of when weight/bias rescaling preserves outputs up to a scalar, and the incorporation of bias rescaling into the procedure is a thoughtful touch often overlooked in ad hoc rescaling methods.
SWR's per-layer, multiplicative shrink toward initialization norms via an EMA-like interpolation is a practical alternative to penalty-based regularization, avoiding additional gradient computations.
The balancedness analysis (mediant argument for norm ratios) is simple and persuasive for the stated metric.

The method is tested across several regimes that are directly relevant to plasticity (warm-start and continual learning in full- and limited-access forms) and also on standard single-task generalization.
Results are given with mean and variance in key tables/figures and use multiple random seeds (5 for small models, 3 for VGG).
The overall motivation is clear: mitigate unbounded weight growth to maintain plasticity without discarding learned information.
The algorithm is concise and easy to implement; the proportionality theorem and boundedness/balancedness statements are readable with intuitive proofs.
Addressing plasticity loss via a simple, optimizer-agnostic post-update operation that does not require extra backward passes is valuable in practical training pipelines.
If robust, the observation that SWR helps larger models (VGG‑16 on TinyImageNet) where standard penalties yield limited gains is of interest to practitioners.
weaknesses: The boundedness guarantee hinges on a strong and somewhat opaque assumption: a uniform bound on the per-step increase of the squared Frobenius norm. While plausible under bounded inputs/gradients, it is neither verified empirically nor justified generically for the considered settings.
The handling of normalization layers under the “proportionality” claim is under-specified. The rule for accumulating bias scaling around normalization is not rigorously justified and appears fragile.

The claim that “SWR recovers plasticity without losing information” is not theoretically substantiated; proportionality preserves the instantaneous output up to a scalar right after rescaling but does not guarantee retention across stages or in the presence of further training.

Baselines are limited relative to the scope of claims. Notably missing are spectral norm/Parseval regularization, AdamW with tuned decay, recent principled reinitialization like FIRE, and other weight-rescaling/balancing schemes.
The S&P baseline is used in a simplified form, which prior work notes can underperform more carefully tuned variants; this may disadvantage reinitialization baselines.

Hyperparameter fairness is insufficiently detailed for SWR vs. baselines; SWR uses separate λf/λc, while other methods are tuned with a single λ. Sensitivity of SWR to λ and scheduling is not deeply analyzed.
The experimental reporting lacks standard training details (data augmentation, scheduler specifics across all regimes), and only 3 seeds are used for VGG-16, limiting statistical reliability.

Algorithm 1's bias-scaling accumulation rule around the “final normalization layer” is confusing (the update of C and when it resets) and should be formalized with a precise derivation for common BN/LN placements.

The statement “two proportional networks have identical behavior in classification tasks” should clarify that argmax of logits is invariant to positive scaling (and note exceptions for threshold-based binary decisions or calibrated metrics).

Close connections to “weight rescaling” during training (e.g., Niehaus et al., 2024) and recent reinitialization/regularization targeting plasticity (e.g., FIRE, Elsayed et al., 2024; spectral/Parseval regularization) are not compared experimentally.

Broader literature on balancing layer magnitudes (Path-SGD, weight-balancing flows, AdamW/decoupled decay scaling rules) suggests complementary or competing approaches that merit discussion and, ideally, empirical comparison.

strengths: Using the explicit flow-matching velocity to induce pairwise couplings via reverse-time integration is an interesting idea that connects FM theory to a direct one-step mapping regime.
The introduction of a small sigma in the conditional map to recover invertibility near t=1 and enable reverse-time integration is well-motivated and neatly explained.
The approach is modular and, in principle, compatible with alternative conditional interpolants and conditional settings (labels).
The toy experiments are appropriate for sanity checks and qualitatively illustrate the role of σ and solver tolerance on the geometry of prototypes.
The color-transfer demo creatively applies the idea beyond canonical generative modeling, showing the pairing concept can be repurposed.
The high-level algorithm is described succinctly, and the role of the buffer for importance sampling is clear.
The paper's intuition for why sigma>0 is needed (invertibility at t≈1) is easy to follow.
Reducing sampling latency to a single forward pass is practically important, and methods that connect flow-based theory to one-step generation are of clear community interest.
If made to scale, a route to amortize FM into one-step generators without iterative distillation could be meaningful.
weaknesses: The method still requires solving reverse-time ODEs during training to compute prototypes for each target sample and iteration; the true training-time cost is not quantified and could be substantial.
The self-normalized importance sampling used to approximate integrals in the explicit velocity is biased; the paper neither characterizes this bias nor shows its practical impact across dimensions.
Numerical stability near t=1 remains a concern; the proposed sigma>0 helps locally, but the paper does not analyze stiffness or error propagation in reverse-time integration.
Some claims (e.g., “almost monotonic” pairings or “segments almost never intersect”) are stated without formal criteria, quantitative evidence, or proofs.
No quantitative evaluation on standard image benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet-64/256) or metrics (FID, IS, CLIP, precision/recall), and no runtime/latency comparisons to strong baselines.

The MNIST and color-transfer results are purely qualitative and very limited in scope; the paper does not compare against strong one-step baselines (e.g., InstaFlow, consistency models/trajectory models, progressive distillation, OT-CFM variants).

There is no ablation on buffer size N, sigma, ODE tolerance, or reverse integration step count, nor analysis of the quality of prototype distributions versus rho0 or variance of the importance weights.
Absent stress tests in higher dimensions, it is unclear whether the importance-sampled explicit velocity and reverse integration remain accurate or stable.
Notation overload: vθ is called a “model representing the velocity,” but is ultimately trained as a direct mapping x0→x1; this is potentially confusing.
Some derivations and assumptions (e.g., invertibility implications, distributional smoothing due to sigma, prototype distribution approximating rho0) are stated informally without rigorous support.
Typos and minor grammar issues (e.g., “prove-of-concept”), and some figures are described but not reproducible without metrics.

Omits or does not compare to major one-step or few-step acceleration literature: consistency models/CTM, InstaFlow, progressive distillation and DMD, fast RF variants (e.g., RF-based one-step I2I like TReFT), and improved FM couplings (e.g., NFM).
Does not situate the approach against OT-based couplings used to train deterministic generators or methods that learn amortized inverses/inversions.

papers.title: Analog In-memory Training on General Non-ideal Resistive Elements: The Impact of Response Functions

strengths: Introduces a general, device-agnostic discrete-time analog update model that separates symmetric and asymmetric response components (F and G), going beyond prior analyses restricted to special linear forms.
Identifies and formalizes an implicit penalty induced by asymmetric responses via a penalized objective f(W) + <Σ, Rc(W)>, providing a clear lens to understand bias and drift toward symmetric points.
Proposes a principled Residual Learning framework cast as a bilevel optimization problem; shows how it subsumes Tiki-Taka and, with zero-shift, can eliminate asymptotic error.
Provides extensions (RL v2) with a digital buffer and thresholding to practically mitigate noisy I/O and finite pulse granularity, aligning algorithm design with hardware realities.
Uses AIHWKIT simulations across multiple architectures (FCN, CNN, ResNets) and datasets (MNIST, CIFAR10/100), showing consistent improvements over Analog SGD and competitive results vs. Tiki-Taka.
Provides a counterexample where small deviations from special linear responses degrade Tiki-Taka, motivating the need for a generic analysis.
The decomposition into F (symmetric) and G (asymmetric) components and the role of the ratio G/√F are clearly explained and connected to convergence behavior.
The connection between the residual-array dynamics and transfer steps is clearly laid out, with intuitive interpretations (alignment of stationary and symmetric points).
Addresses a central challenge in on-chip analog training: biased and asymmetric updates that can systematically hinder convergence.
Offers theory-backed guidance for device/algorithm co-design (e.g., importance of zero-shifting), valuable to both AIMC algorithm developers and hardware designers.
weaknesses: The approximate update model Uq(w, Δw) linearizes the per-pulse accumulation and assumes Δw is effectively scaled by q±(w), neglecting intra-burst state evolution and per-pulse nonlinearity; the conditions under which this approximation is accurate are not fully characterized.

Exact convergence requires the zero-shift condition G(0) = 0 and additional Lipschitz assumptions; sensitivity to calibration errors in zero-shift and device-to-device variability of symmetric points is not analyzed.

Some theoretical constants and conditions (e.g., γ ≥ Ω(q_min^(-2/5))) appear without intuition or derivation in the main text, and the reliance on strong convexity for uniqueness (Theorem 3) limits generality for deep networks.

Experiments primarily use response functions that already satisfy the zero-shift assumption, aligning well with the theory but leaving open how robust RL is to mis-specified or drifting symmetric points.

Limited comparisons to other mitigation approaches (e.g., mixed-precision digital accumulation schemes) and lack of hardware overhead/energy analysis for maintaining an extra analog array and a digital buffer.
Missing ablations on the impact of device-to-device variability in response functions, as well as robustness to errors in estimating F and G.
The definition of Σ in Theorem 1 (apparently an elementwise first moment of the gradient noise at W*) is not crisply specified in the main text; double-bracket notation is unclear.
Several proofs are deferred; while acceptable, key intuitions (e.g., why the bias can be captured as <Σ, Rc(W)>) could be expanded in the main body for accessibility.

Limited discussion of mixed-precision or digital-accumulation approaches that also mitigate non-ideal updates (e.g., Nandakumar et al. 2017/2020), which are conceptually related to the buffer-based RL v2 transfer and could serve as strong baselines.

Other non-idealities (IR-drop, ADC/DAC nonlinearity, device drift) are recognized but not integrated into either theory or experiments; related experimental emulation and programming-improvement works (e.g., GDP) could be discussed for context.

papers.title: Why Diffusion Models Don't Memorize: The Role of Implicit Dynamical Regularization in Training

strengths: The discovery and quantification of two distinct, training-dynamics timescales in diffusion models—τgen (quality) and τmem (memorization)—and the linear scaling law τmem ∝ n (with τgen essentially n-independent) is a crisp, novel contribution.

The random-features analysis rigorously ties these timescales to the eigen-spectrum of a data-dependent correlation matrix, showing two well-separated bulks (ρ2 for generalization and ρ1 for memorization) and providing an analytic mechanism for dynamical regularization.
The scaling with model capacity (τgen ∝ 1/W, τmem ∝ n/W) offers a practical, quantitative bridge between architecture and training dynamics.

Experiments systematically vary dataset size n and model capacity p (via width W), measure FID and a standardized nearest-neighbor memorization score, and observe excellent collapse under the proposed rescalings (τ/n for memorization; Wτ for quality).
The study includes controls to disentangle optimizer dynamics from data repetition (e.g., full-batch checks showing τmem ∝ n persists), strengthening the causal interpretation.
Training/test loss traces at fixed diffusion time demonstrate overfitting onset scaling with n and lag relative to observed memorization—consistent with the theory.
The conceptual picture (phase diagram in n–p, training-time windows, and evolving learned score vs. empirical/population scores) is compelling and consistently referenced across sections.
Theoretical assumptions are stated, and the link between spectral properties and timescales is clearly articulated.

Understanding and mitigating memorization in diffusion models is important for privacy, safety, and scientific use; the identified dynamical regularization window delivers actionable training guidance (early stopping, capacity control).
The random-features framework advances theoretical understanding of training-time phenomena beyond τ → ∞ analyses, complementing existing asymptotic generalization/memorization results.
weaknesses: The theory relies on a random-features model with Gaussian/sub-Gaussian assumptions and fixed-time DSM; extrapolation to deep U-Nets with time-conditioning and complex data distributions is plausible but not guaranteed.
Some derivations use replica methods (non-rigorous) and asymptotic limits; constants and finite-size effects relevant in practice are not fully characterized.
The mapping between SGD steps τ and the continuous-time limit’s τ (scaled by η and d) is not calibrated to yield predictive constants for real models.

Empirical validation is restricted to low-resolution, grayscale CelebA and unconditional models; broader datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet subsets, or color CelebA-HQ) and modalities would strengthen generality.

Only one primary memorization metric (nearest-neighbor ratio with fixed threshold) is reported; complementary measures (e.g., SSCD similarity or attack-based extraction metrics) would increase robustness.
The main text largely focuses on SGD; Adam and other optimizers are delegated to the supplement, and the dependence of scaling on batch size and learning rate is not systematically studied.

A few equation typos/inconsistencies (e.g., notational slips around τgen/mem, occasional missing parentheses/powers, eq. (19) formatting) may hinder reproducibility of the theoretical part without the SM.
The definition and identification procedure for τgen could be stated more precisely in the main text (e.g., exact FID criterion/time of minimum).

While prior works on memorization and spectral bias are discussed, alignment with recent security/privacy extraction studies (e.g., surrogate conditioning SIDE attacks) and anti-memorization training schemes (e.g., IET‑AGC) could be deepened to delineate when dynamical regularization suffices versus when dedicated defenses are needed.
Connections to recent geometric analyses of sampling-time phases could be elaborated to contrast training-time vs sampling-time mechanisms.

papers.title: Mechanistic Insights into Grokking from the Embedding Layer

strengths: The paper spotlights a plausible, under-explored mechanism for grokking centered on embedding dynamics and bilinear coupling with downstream weights, and connects this to broader optimization issues in Transformers.
The proposed LR ratio c ∝ σmax(E)/σmax(W) · fW/fE is a concise, interpretable heuristic for balancing coupled updates under bilinear interactions.

The study includes multiple algorithmic tasks (addition, multiplication, division, sum of squares mod p), batch-size sweeps, and sampling strategies (uniform, random, skewed), providing an initial empirical landscape.
Curvature diagnostics via Hessian-vector products and singular-value analyses are informative and tie back to the coupling hypothesis.

The high-level narrative—grokking via embedding update imbalance and bilinear coupling—is intuitive and well-motivated, with visualizations (e.g., “trapezoid” grokking curves, gradient heatmaps) supporting the story.

If rigorously substantiated, the paper’s embedding-centric lens could influence how we design sampling strategies and optimizer hyperparameters for models with large embedding tables, including Transformers.
weaknesses: The “Proposition 4.1” and the claim that the adaptive LR ratio “mitigates bilinear coupling” are argued via proportional scaling intuition but lack a precise, rigorous convergence or stability analysis; assumptions and scope are not clearly stated.
The use of a constant c=10 in experiments undercuts the “adaptive” premise, and the dependence on σmax and update frequencies is not operationalized online.
Several derivations contain inconsistencies (e.g., ODE solution and symbols in Eq. 9; dimensions for W in Appendix B) and the smoothness-based inequalities (Eqs. 5–6) are imprecise.

The comparison “MLP with vs without embeddings” is not controlled for capacity, input representation, and parameter count, making causal attribution to embeddings unclear. The “without embeddings” case encodes a and b as real-valued inputs, which is structurally different from a one-hot or learned-embedding setup.

Frequency-aware “sampling” is largely implemented via train/test splits rather than per-mini-batch balancing; it’s unclear whether per-batch token exposure is actually controlled, which is central to the p_i hypothesis.
No multi-seed reporting, statistical significance, or reproducibility details (code, exact hyperparameters, p values) are provided; several figures are qualitative without quantitative tables.
Use of weight decay as a key factor is asserted, but ablations with λ=0 vs >0 (and varying λ) are thin relative to the central claim.
Numerous typographical errors and notation inconsistencies impede understanding (e.g., “TheThe,” inconsistent use of g/q in Eq. 9, dimensions of W, missing terms/typos in inequalities).
Some datasets (e.g., division mod p) are not precisely specified. Several figure captions and references are placeholders or ambiguous.

The idea of per-parameter learning-rate groups (e.g., higher LR for embeddings) is widely used; the novelty claim would benefit from deeper positioning against works on blockwise curvature heterogeneity and parameter-scaling views of adaptive optimizers.

The connection to bilinear saddle-point optimization and spectral conditioning could be more formally grounded by leveraging existing convex–concave bilinear analyses; current links remain largely qualitative.

papers.title: Beyond Self-Attention: A Subquadratic Fourier-Wavelet Transformer with Multi-Modal Fusion

strengths: Combining Fourier mixing (global, parameter-free) with wavelet transforms (localized, multiresolution) in a unified Transformer block is a compelling architectural idea that brings complementary inductive biases.
The proposed adaptive frequency bases and learnable scale weights are natural extensions that could enhance expressivity over fixed spectral bases.
Pursuing a general encoder–decoder design (including cross-conditioning) fully “beyond attention” is ambitious and timely given the cost of O(N^2) attention.
The paper includes an ablation study isolating the contribution of learned frequencies, adaptive scales, and a basic multimodal fusion signal.
Training details (optimizer, schedule, batch size, model dimensionality) are provided at a high level for reproducibility.
The motivation and background situate the work among FNet and efficient-attention literature, and the narrative is generally easy to follow.
Complexity targets and intended benefits are clearly stated, with a limitations section that acknowledges trade-offs.
Reducing long-context cost without approximating attention scores is of practical interest, and spectral-wavelet designs are a promising direction for long-input summarization and beyond.
If correct and generalizable, the proposed subquadratic, attention-free encoder–decoder could widen accessibility of long-context models.
weaknesses: The “causal masking” for FFT-based mixing is not specified with a concrete, subquadratic algorithm; the presented triangular time-domain mask appears to imply O(N^2) computation unless an efficient algorithmic trick is given. As written, the masked sum is content- and index-dependent (w(n,k)), not a stationary convolution amenable to a single FFT.

The Fourier cross-mixing formulation is dimensionally unclear and its semantics are not well-justified; it mixes Q and K via a concatenated FFT and multiplies by V^T, but shapes and the role of Q/K/V in conditioning are not rigorously derived. It is not obvious that this operation captures content-based alignment comparable to cross-attention.

The wavelet branch description has dimensional inconsistencies (e.g., W ∈ R^(JM)×d and P ∈ R^d×(JM) with X̃ = W P^T claimed to be in R^(N×d)). It is unclear how wavelet coefficients are mapped back to N-length sequences without an explicit inverse DWT (or how P replaces iDWT).

The model largely performs fixed linear mixing in the sequence dimension; while gates and learned bases help, this may be insufficient to replace the expressivity of content-dependent attention for many tasks.

Dataset mismatch and clarity: “PubMed 200K RCT” is a sentence classification dataset, not the standard long-document summarization (“PubMed” from Cohan et al., 2018). The paper uses ROUGE and long inputs, suggesting summarization, but the dataset name/protocol appears inconsistent.

Baselines are narrow: comparisons only to FNet variants and LED; modern subquadratic baselines (e.g., Mamba/SSMs, Hyena, LongVQ, FLASH, Poolingformer, BigBird) are not included, limiting the empirical support for claims.

Fairness of LED baseline unclear: LED-base-16384 is typically pretrained with dimension 768; the paper fixes d=512 for all models—unclear whether LED is matched in capacity and fine-tuned under the same regimen.

Statistical rigor: The main tables lack error bars/CI despite text claiming bootstrap CIs. The checklist contradicts itself (first saying CIs are reported, then stating “No” due to compute constraints).
Multimodal setup is under-specified (what exactly is the second modality, how aligned, how masked), and the fusion is simple concatenation before FFT without deeper analysis.
Several mathematical derivations are informal or contain dimensional inconsistencies (wavelet mapping back to token space; cross-mixing dimensions; causal mask efficiency).
The causal “inverse FFT” wording vs forward FFT is inconsistent in places.

Omits key recent alternatives for long-context modeling (state-space models like Mamba and S4, hybrid attention-memory approaches, quantized-key attention like LongVQ, mixed-chunk attention like FLASH, and other wavelet-transformer variants such as MSWT). Discussion and empirical comparisons are needed to establish novelty and significance within the current landscape.

papers.title: SWE-Lancer: Can Frontier LLMs Earn $1 Million from Real-World Freelance Software Engineering?

strengths: Uses execution-based, end-to-end tests tailored to each task by professional engineers, reducing susceptibility to unit-test “grader hacking” and better approximating full-stack, user-visible correctness.
Introduces SWE Manager tasks grounded in real proposal selection, expanding evaluation beyond code patching to technical leadership and judgment.
Maps capability to monetary value using actual payouts, creating a pragmatic, market-derived difficulty signal and enabling cost-benefit analyses.
Provides a unified, offline Docker harness with codebase snapshots reverted pre-fix and a “user tool” for self-testing via browser automation.
Thorough baseline evaluation across models and ablations (pass@k, test-time compute, user tool enablement) with both pass rates and “dollars earned.”
Triple verification of E2E tests and an automated check that tests pass in the fixed state and fail in the reverted state; 99% agreement for managerial-choice validation with experienced engineers.
Careful contamination mitigation in the harness (no internet; commit history constraints), plus a preliminary contamination analysis by cutoff date.
Clear articulation of task construction, evaluation protocol, and economic framing, with concrete examples and figures explaining flows and categories.
Transparent limitations section (single repository, text-only evaluation, no interactive clarifications) and thoughtful discussion of potential contamination.
Addresses a pressing gap: realistic, full-stack, economically grounded SWE evaluation that remains unsaturated for frontier models.
The management dimension and monetary mapping make the benchmark highly relevant for research on agentic systems, labor economics, and safe deployment.
weaknesses: Single product/repository (Expensify) and a largely JS/TS front-end codebase limit ecological diversity (e.g., infra/DevOps, systems, data engineering).

Binary payout scoring (all-or-nothing) may overstate “earned value” relative to the multifaceted work originally done to close issues; it under-credits partial progress and overlooks code quality/security/style.

Ground truth for SWE Manager tasks equates success with matching the ultimately accepted proposal, which may not be the only technically satisfactory option; although the 99% validation helps, the framing remains inherently non-unique.
Results for open-source models are relegated to the appendix; main text focuses on a narrow set of proprietary models, limiting broader community reproducibility and comparison.

Limited statistical treatment for pass@1 single runs on large subsets (noted due to rate limits), making small differences potentially noisy; more seeds or CIs on primary results would strengthen claims.
The user tool is text-only, yet parts of the paper imply models can “ingest screenshots”; elsewhere it states models cannot view screenshots, yielding a confusing multimodality picture.

Minor inconsistencies around multimodal capabilities and the user tool outputs (screenshots saved vs. models’ inability to view them) may confuse readers about what feedback signals models actually receive.

The economic analysis is helpful but mixes experimental results with assumptions (e.g., pass@5, verification latencies) and historical rates; the caveats are acknowledged, but the narrative could better separate empirical findings from speculative scenarios.

While the paper compares against common code benchmarks (HumanEval, SWE-Bench, etc.), it could more directly situate itself among recent end-to-end, agentic, or web-app/full-stack evaluations (e.g., Vision2Web, Vibe Code Bench, DevBench, ReCodeAgent) that also emphasize execution-based or workflow verification.
Discussion of test flakiness, determinism across environments, and evaluator reliability could borrow methodology from recent agent-verification works.

strengths: Framing multi-turn, partially observable text tasks as sources for self-generated preference data is a practical and general approach to instilling exploration and adaptation without task-specific algorithms.
Use of a sequential DPO formulation that focuses loss on agent tokens is well-motivated for multi-turn interactions and aligns with recent advances in preference optimization for trajectories.
The curriculum component introduces a simple, model-agnostic selection signal (coefficient of variation) and an efficient bandit-style allocation across task groups.

A relatively broad suite of 10 task groups spans guessing games, programmatically verifiable games (Wordle, Minesweeper, Battleship, Mastermind), a toy scientific inference setting (Cellular Automata), customer-service style dialogues, and bandit best-arm identification.
Leave-one-out (LOO) studies probe cross-task generalization, and single-group vs. multi-group comparisons test for transfer vs. in-group learning.
The paper tests two base models (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Gemma-3-12B-IT) and evaluates standard benchmarks (MT-Bench, AlpacaEval, MMLU-Pro, GPQA, Math) to monitor non-degradation.
The problem setup, task suite design criteria, and optimization objectives (SFT, DPO, RPO) are explained clearly with helpful intuition.
The empirical section is well-structured around key questions: generalization, curriculum efficacy, and impact on standard capabilities.
The release of code/data/models (per the reproducibility statement) adds clarity and credibility.
The paper tackles an important gap: learning general, reusable multi-turn exploration/interaction strategies rather than task-specific solvers.
Evidence of zero-shot transfer to unseen task groups (notably for bandits without expert trajectories) is promising and practically relevant for agentic LLM research.
The approach emphasizes data collection as the bottleneck and proposes a scalable selection method—this reorientation is timely given the cost structure in LLM post-training.
weakness: The curriculum signal νπ(τ) = std/mean can be unstable when the mean is small; no smoothing/clipping details are provided, and there is limited analysis of estimator variance under small C.

The UCB procedure is over coarse “groups,” and in at least one demonstration (Twenty Questions) groupings come from GPT-4o-mini difficulty labels. This relies on heuristic and potentially noisy metadata.

The method largely reuses known components (self-play sampling, DPO/RPO, bandit allocation) and the novelty lies in composition and scope; theoretical underpinnings (e.g., when/why preference optimization learned on many tasks yields amortized exploration) are not developed.

Ablations are missing: contributions of SFT vs. DPO vs. RPO are not disentangled; the importance of min-p sampling vs. standard top-p/temperature; and the effect of including/excluding chain-of-thought across tasks is unclear.

Reliance on LLM environments/judges risks label noise or subtle leakage. While the paper mentions mitigations and hardcoded verifiers for some tasks, rigorous auditing of failure/edge cases is limited in the main text.
The bandit best-arm identification test set has size 1 (Table 1), raising concerns about robustness and overinterpretation of a large reported improvement.
Comparisons to alternative preference-tuning baselines (e.g., RAFT, KTO) or online RLHF/PPO variants on a subset of tasks are absent, leaving open whether DPO/RPO is the most effective choice.
Algorithmic details are sometimes light: specifics for β in DPO, numerical stability for νπ(τ), and how pass@k and formatting failures are handled per task could be clearer in the main text.
Some figures provide only high-level summaries; confidence intervals and per-task variance are mostly deferred to appendices.

Limited discussion of recent multi-turn agent training with online RL or expert-iteration pipelines (e.g., RL on reasoning tasks, expert iteration beyond brief citations) and how PAPRIKA’s offline preference tuning compares in sample and compute efficiency.
The paper does not engage with fine-grained capability diagnostics (e.g., cognitive-diagnostic frameworks) that could help characterize what “general decision-making” abilities are learned.

papers.title: Multivariate Conformal Prediction using Optimal Transport

strengths: Uses a principled notion of multivariate ranking (center-outward/Kantorovich ranks) to address a core difficulty in multivariate CP: ordering vector-valued scores without ad hoc scalarization.

Connects the population concept (Brenier map to uniform ball) to a tractable implementation with entropic OT maps and a discrete spherical grid, accompanied by ablations over regularization and grid size.
Clearly situates the approach as a generalization of norm-based scalarizations (e.g., Mahalanobis); this link is insightful and helps interpret behavior.
Broad benchmark based on Dheur et al. (2025), spanning multiple output dimensions (2–16) and dataset sizes, with comparisons against M-CP and Merge-CP (L2 and Mahalanobis).
Sensible ablations on the two key hyperparameters (entropic regularization ε and number of target points m), including coverage and runtime analyses.
The intuition for OT-based ranks/quantiles is accessible and motivated via the one-dimensional probability integral transform.
The practical section on entropic map estimation (dual formulation, out-of-sample mapping, complexity) is useful for practitioners.
Multivariate CP is an important, timely problem with active interest; a flexible, distribution-free route to ranking multivariate scores is valuable.
The approach could catalyze further work on combining OT depth/ranks with CP in higher dimensions and in structured settings.
weaknesses: The main finite-sample guarantee effectively follows standard split conformal logic once a fixed transformation T̂ is chosen: the transformed norms are just another univariate nonconformity score. The paper’s claim of “first calibration guarantee … for any approximation map T” downplays that conformal validity under arbitrary (fixed) nonconformity scores is classical.

Some theoretical parts are unclear or potentially problematic: several arguments rely on a map T_{n+1} built from the n+1 sample including the test point, or on rα(T̂, P) defined with respect to the unknown P, and then pivot to the empirical version. The connection to implementable, data-split conformal procedures could be clarified and tightened.

OT map estimation in moderate/high dimensions is known to be statistically and computationally challenging; beyond heuristic guidance (ε, m), there is little discussion of stability or failure modes when the map is poorly estimated.

Missing comparisons to other multivariate CP methods that do not require full generative models but are relevant, e.g., CD-split/HPD-split (Izbicki et al., 2022), recent copula-based conformal methods, or center-outward depth baselines not relying on entropic maps (e.g., semi-discrete assignment).

Evaluation of region size relies on importance sampling from MQF² for test-time metrics, while OT-CP itself assumes only a point predictor; this asymmetry could bias the efficiency metric and should be justified, stress-tested, or complemented by generator-free proxies (e.g., set volume/coverage on synthetic data with known ground truth).
Hyperparameter selection strategy for ε and m across datasets is not fully specified; it is unclear whether tuning used calibration data in a way that preserves coverage guarantees.

Several notation and equation references are inconsistent or contain rendering artifacts (e.g., Equation (7), self-referential figure captions, occasional typos), which impede precise understanding of the coverage claims.

The roles of T*, T̂, T_{n+1}, and their training/calibration splits are not always distinct; the implementation discussion suggests a fixed T̂ learned from residuals, but some proofs refer to objects that depend on the unseen test point.

Recent work using OT-based center-outward ranks within conformal prediction appears closely related (e.g., multivariate conformal predictive distributions via augmented-sample OT ranks). A more explicit comparison of assumptions, guarantees, and computational trade-offs (e.g., recomputing OT per candidate vs. a fixed map) is needed.
Copula-based and other multivariate distribution-free conformal approaches are mentioned but not thoroughly compared empirically or theoretically, despite being natural baselines.

papers.title: Training Language Models to Reason Efficiently

strengths: The reward shaping is minimalist and practical: a per-prompt, bounded length penalty on correct rollouts encourages early stopping without compromising correctness.

The per-prompt normalization addresses the central pitfall of naively penalizing length across problems of different difficulty, aligning the objective with “shortest correct conditional on problem difficulty.”
The ablation that eschews advantage normalization is insightful, with a clear argument and empirical evidence that normalization can induce over-shrinking independent of α.
Demonstrates consistent compute–accuracy trade-offs across two open-weight reasoning models and three benchmarks of varying difficulty.
Shows that alpha provides smooth control of the cost/accuracy Pareto front and that a very short RL run suffices to realize savings, highlighting practical viability.
The method is explained concisely with a clear objective, intuitive diagrams, and implementation details (sampling, PPO with RLOO, batch sizes, hardware).
The training dynamics analysis for different α values and the advantage-normalization ablation are easy to follow.
The problem—reducing inference-time cost for chain-of-thought models—is impactful for deployment, latency, and sustainability.
The approach is orthogonal to system-level and model-level compression and can be combined with them.
Producing a family of checkpoints with a single scalar control is practically valuable.
weaknesses: Reward sparsity: only correct rollouts receive nonzero reward. For hard prompts (e.g., AIME), per-prompt mean/std among “correct” may be undefined or highly noisy, but the paper does not specify the handling of zero-correct cases or few-correct statistics.

The per-prompt statistics are computed from on-policy samples at each iteration, introducing non-stationarity and variance; stability strategies (e.g., moving averages, minimum sample thresholds) are not discussed.

The claim that attention cost is “quadratic” during inference is imprecise; decoding cost per token grows roughly linearly with context length using KV caching, which matters for interpreting speedups.
No evaluation beyond math/verifiable tasks; the approach depends on outcome verifiers and its generality to other domains is not demonstrated.

Limited baselines: strong inference-time baselines such as verifier-driven early stopping, best-of-N with length-aware selection, or process-level rewards (e.g., RePro-style RLVR augmentations) are missing.
Results are mainly visual (scatter plots) without aggregated numeric tables, confidence intervals, or multiple seeds; reproducibility and statistical robustness are hard to assess.
Pass@k definition and compute accounting are underspecified. It is unclear whether “pass rate@k” is any-correct or average-of-k, and how token counts are aggregated across k samples and prompts.

Important implementation details are absent: reference policy for KL control, exact RL objective and clipping settings, handling of multi-GPU computation of per-prompt stats, and how missing per-prompt stats are treated.
Some inconsistencies/typos in figure legends and notation (e.g., f(LEN(x)) vs f(LEN(z))) can be confusing.

Concurrent and closely related work on adaptive reflection/length penalties (e.g., ARLCP) and process-level reward shaping (e.g., RePro) are not discussed or compared, despite addressing the same efficiency–accuracy goal with complementary techniques.
Recent work on CoT compression/trustworthiness trade-offs reports that shortening CoT can induce safety/hallucination regressions; this paper does not evaluate those aspects.
